records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Priority Partners (690) MCO Formulary, effective 07/01/2026, pages 35-36, section 'ANTIDIABETICS > INSULIN'. Confirmed via PyMuPDF text extraction of the downloaded PDF (page-1 caption 'Priority Partners (690) / Effective 07/01/2026'). The formulary lists insulin by brand/product code rather than by INN, consistent with insulin's brand-specific coverage norm noted in the class description. Basal insulins (LANTUS/glargine, GLARGIN YFGN biosimilar) and rapid-acting insulins (HUMALOG/lispro, NOVOLOG/aspart, ADMELOG/lispro, FIASP/faster aspart) are all covered with quantity limits only, no PA, for the standard vial and pen delivery forms. A distinct age-based restriction pattern appears specifically on the older human-insulin KwikPen delivery devices (Humulin and Novolin brand pens): covered without restriction for patients under 18, but requiring PA for adults — this is a delivery-device-specific PA, not a molecule-level restriction, and does not apply to the vial forms of the same insulins. Chose the interchangeable glargine biosimilar (marketed as unbranded 'Semglee'/listed as GLARGIN YFGN in the formulary) as the representative preferred basal agent since FDA-designated interchangeable biosimilars are the lowest-cost basal option and are listed ahead of brand Lantus in the document; verification of directions-for-use text is inferred from standard insulin glargine prescribing practice since the formulary itself lists only product name and UM flags, not sigs.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Maryland Medicaid Pharmacy Program Preferred Drug List, Effective Date 7/1/2026, page 4 of 33, section 'ANALGESICS' > 'Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatories (NSAIDS)'. Preferred column (verbatim) lists celecoxib (Celebrex), diclofenac sodium, diclofenac gel (Voltaren Gel), ibuprofen Rx/OTC (Motrin), indomethacin (Indocin), meloxicam tablets (Mobic), nabumetone (Relafen), naproxen, naproxen sodium OTC, and sulindac (Clinoril) -- all with no PA/QL flag; this is an unusually broad preferred list with no single ranked 'top' agent identified by the source. Non-Preferred / Requires Prior Authorization column (verbatim) lists a long roster of alternate NSAIDs and formulations: diclofenac epolamine patch (Flector), diclofenac potassium capsule/tablet, diclofenac/misoprostol (Arthrotec), diclofenac SR (Voltaren XL), diflunisal (Dolobid), etodolac/etodolac XL (Lodine, Lodine XL), fenoprofen, flurbiprofen, ibuprofen 300mg (cc,ql), ibuprofen chewable OTC, ibuprofen/famotidine (Duexis), indomethacin ER (Indocin SR), indomethacin rectal, ketoprofen/ketoprofen ER (Orudis, Oruvail), ketorolac (Toradol), meclofenamate (Meclomen), mefenamic acid (Ponstel), meloxicam capsules (Vivlodex), naproxen/esomeprazole (Vimovo), oxaprozin (Daypro), piroxicam (Feldene), tolmetin sodium, and branded products Coxanto Patch, Licart, Lofena, Relafen DS, Vyscoxa. Naproxen chosen as the representative preferred agent per standard first-line-oral-NSAID convention (matches the class definition's own example ordering); the PDL itself does not distinguish a single top pick among its nine clean-preferred generics. Tier inferred (partial) as standard preferred generic; no explicit tier/copay level stated in the PDL.

records.verificationNote: Fetched member.carefirst.com/carefirst-resources/pdf/exchange-formulary-sum7277-2026.pdf via curl and parsed locally with pypdf (plain WebFetch returned only unparsed binary PDF stream data). Page 1 confirms 'CareFirst Exchange Formulary 2026 ... Last updated 06/01/26'. Pages 1-2 (in-document numbering 1-2), under ANALGESICS > COX-2 INHIBITORS and NSAIDS, list verbatim: celecoxib 50/100/200mg (Tier 1, blank), diclofenac potassium 50mg (Tier 1, blank), diclofenac sodium gel 1% (Tier 1, 'QL (300g every 30 days), OTC'), diclofenac sodium delayed-release/ER tablets (Tier 1, blank), etodolac all forms (Tier 1, blank), flurbiprofen (Tier 1, blank), ibuprofen suspension/tablets (Tier 1, blank), ketorolac tromethamine tab 10mg (Tier 1, 'QL (20 tabs every 30 days)'), meclofenamate sodium, mefenamic acid, meloxicam 7.5/15mg (Tier 1, blank), nabumetone (Tier 1, blank), naproxen 250/375/500mg (Tier 1, blank), oxaprozin, piroxicam, sulindac (all Tier 1, blank), voltaren arthritis pain gel (Tier 1, 'QL (300g every 30 days), OTC'), and the NSAIDS, COMBINATIONS subsection listing diclofenac w/ misoprostol delayed-release 50-0.2mg and 75-0.2mg (Tier 1, blank). Every single oral NSAID in the formulary is Tier 1 with no PA or step-therapy restriction; only ketorolac (a short-course-only NSAID due to GI/renal risk) and the topical diclofenac gel carry quantity limits, which are standard clinical safety guardrails rather than access barriers. Selected naproxen 500mg as the preferred agent per common first-line convention for musculoskeletal pain; the entire class being Tier 1 no-PA means there is no meaningfully differentiated 'preferred' vs 'alternative' status -- all are equally accessible, captured as alternatives. Confirmed by full-document case-insensitive search that no brand NSAID (Celebrex is absent as a brand name -- generic celecoxib is listed only; Mobic, Anaprox, Naprosyn, Voltaren Gel is present as a specific branded OTC product line item alongside generic diclofenac gel) creates any PA/ST barrier; there is no drug in the requested oral-NSAID list that is excluded, so paRequired is empty. No class-wide step-therapy or BOGL language found near this section.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Priority Partners (690) MCO Formulary, effective 07/01/2026, pages 21-22, section 'NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENTS (NSAIDS)' (nested under the ANALGESICS - ANTI-INFLAMMATORY parent heading). Confirmed via PyMuPDF text extraction of the downloaded PDF (page-1 caption 'Priority Partners (690) / Effective 07/01/2026'). Every single oral NSAID entry in this section — naproxen (all salts/forms), ibuprofen (all forms), meloxicam, celecoxib, diclofenac (both salts, all release forms), etodolac, flurbiprofen, indomethacin, ketorolac, mefenamic acid, nabumetone, oxaprozin, and sulindac — is listed with zero PA/ST/QL/nonformulary flag in the extracted text; this is a fully open, all-generic-covered class with no gating whatsoever. Naproxen 500mg chosen as representative preferred agent as the most commonly prescribed first-line oral NSAID for musculoskeletal pain per clinical convention (this text-based sig is inferred from standard prescribing information, not printed verbatim in the formulary, which lists only drug name).

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the UnitedHealthcare 2026 PDL, effective January 1, 2026, page 8 (Analgesics - Drugs for Pain and Inflammation). Confirmed: 'meloxicam oral tablet 1', 'naproxen oral tablet 1', 'naproxen oral tablet delayed release 1', 'ibuprofen oral tablet 400 mg, 600 mg, 800 mg 1', 'diclofenac sodium oral 1', 'nabumetone oral 1', 'sulindac oral 1', 'indomethacin oral capsule 1', 'ketorolac tromethamine oral 1', 'ec-naproxen 1' -- all Tier 1. Confirmed Tier 2: 'celecoxib oral 2', 'naproxen sodium oral tablet 275 mg, 550 mg 2', 'etodolac 2', 'oxaprozin oral tablet 2', 'piroxicam oral 2', 'indomethacin er 2', 'diclofenac potassium oral tablet 50 mg 2'. Confirmed PA/exclusion-flagged ('E'): 'CELEBREX E', 'NAPROSYN E', 'ANAPROX DS E', 'LODINE E', 'RELAFEN DS E', 'diclofenac potassium oral tablet 25 mg E QL', 'LOFENA E QL', 'ibuprofen oral suspension 100 mg/5ml E'. Confirmed Tier 3/4 non-preferred: 'diclofenac sodium er 3', 'diclofenac-misoprostol 3', 'EC-NAPROSYN ORAL TABLET DELAYED RELEASE 375 MG 3', 'EC-NAPROSYN ORAL TABLET DELAYED RELEASE 500 MG 4', 'DAYPRO 4', 'FELDENE ORAL CAPSULE 20 MG 4'. This class is entirely generic-dominated with no PA/step therapy on any Tier 1/2 generic -- consistent with oral NSAIDs being OTC-adjacent, low-cost first-line agents. Sig reflects standard meloxicam FDA-label dosing, not stated verbatim in the PDL.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Cigna Healthcare Value 4-Tier Prescription Drug List, coverage starting 07/01/2026 (list last updated 04/01/2026), page 22 of 33, section 'PAIN RELIEF AND INFLAMMATORY DISEASE'. Confirmed verbatim: 'celecoxib 1 QL', 'diclofenac 1% gel, tablet 1 QL', 'indomethacin capsule, oral suspension, 50 mg suppository 1', 'meloxicam tablet 1', 'nabumetone 1', and 'ketorolac syringe, tablet, vial 1 QL' all appear at Tier 1 with no PA/ST beyond the noted QL on some agents. A full-document text search for 'naproxen' and 'ibuprofen' (both generic and any brand variant) returned zero matches across all 33 pages -- these two of the most commonly prescribed oral NSAIDs are entirely absent from this drug list, which is consistent with the document's own stated policy (page 3) of excluding certain OTC-available drug classes from prescription coverage, though that page-3 note explicitly names only allergy and heartburn OTC classes, not NSAIDs by name -- flagging this inference as the least-certain part of this record. Meloxicam selected as representative preferred agent (once-daily dosing, common first-line oral NSAID choice) among the multiple clean Tier-1 options; the source itself does not rank within the Tier-1 generics. Representative strength and standard once-daily sig for meloxicam 15 mg are standard clinical convention, not stated verbatim in the PDL (drug/tier/notes format only); tier level is read directly from the document.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the '2026 Aetna Pharmacy Drug Guide - Aetna Standard Plan' PDF (doc code 1348063-01-04 (01/26), effective January 2026). ANALGESICS > NSAIDS section (page 3/internal page 1) lists verbatim: 'diclofenac sodium, diclofenac sodium gel 1%, diclofenac sodium solution 1.5%, ibuprofen, meloxicam tablet, naproxen (except naproxen CR or naproxen suspension)'; the adjacent COX-2 INHIBITORS subsection lists 'celecoxib'; NSAIDS, COMBINATIONS lists 'diclofenac sodium-misoprostol, ibuprofen-famotidine'. The PREFERRED OPTIONS LIST FOR FORMULARY DRUG REMOVALS section confirms CELEBREX is removed, redirected verbatim to 'celecoxib, diclofenac sodium, ibuprofen, meloxicam tablet, naproxen (except naproxen CR or naproxen suspension)', and separately confirms multiple diclofenac-salt-form removal entries (diclofenac potassium capsule/powder) redirected to the sodium-salt generic tablet forms, plus ARTHROTEC redirected to an NSAID-plus-PPI combination. Naproxen (generic, IR tablet) was selected as representative preferred agent among the several equally-listed oral NSAID generics since it is a first-line guideline choice with favorable cardiovascular safety profile among NSAIDs; the Drug Guide does not rank the generics against each other. Sig/strength are representative standard naproxen dosing, not printed verbatim in the Drug Guide (which lists drug names only, no dosing) - marked inferred.

records.verificationNote: Fetched the AARP Medicare Rx Preferred from UHC (PDP) Complete Drug List (Formulary) 2026 PDF via curl (plain WebFetch 403'd; server-side curl with browser User-Agent succeeded, HTTP 200, application/pdf), extracted full text locally with PyMuPDF. Cover page confirms Formulary ID 00026000, Last updated October 1, 2025. Read verbatim from the 'Analgesics > Nonsteroidal Anti-inflammatory Drugs' subsection (PDF page 30, extracted lines 2149-2226): 'Celecoxib' G Tier 3 QL; 'Diclofenac Potassium (50MG)' G Tier 2 no code; 'Diclofenac Sodium ER' G Tier 2 no code; 'Diclofenac Sodium (1.5% External Solution)' G Tier 3 PA; 'Diclofenac Sodium (Oral Tablet Delayed Release)' G Tier 2 no code; 'Diflunisal' G Tier 3 no code; 'Etodolac ER' G Tier 3 no code; 'Etodolac (capsule)' G Tier 3 no code; 'Etodolac (immediate-release tablet)' G Tier 3 no code; 'Flurbiprofen' G Tier 2 no code; 'Ibu (600/800mg)' G Tier 1 no code; 'Ibuprofen (100mg/5mL suspension)' G Tier 1 no code; 'Ibuprofen (400/600/800mg tablet)' G Tier 1 no code; 'Indomethacin ER' G Tier 3 no code; 'Indomethacin (immediate release)' G Tier 2 no code; 'Ketorolac Tromethamine' G Tier 3 no code; 'Meloxicam' G Tier 1 no code; 'Nabumetone' G Tier 2 no code; 'Naproxen DR' G Tier 2 no code; 'Naproxen (immediate release)' G Tier 1 no code; 'Naproxen (375mg DR, Generic EC-Naprosyn)' G Tier 2 no code; 'Naproxen Sodium' G Tier 2 no code; 'Sulindac' G Tier 2 no code. This entire class is 100% generic (no brand NSAID appears anywhere in the document) with only one restriction in the whole subsection (topical diclofenac requiring PA). Naproxen (immediate release) is chosen as the representative preferred agent alongside ibuprofen and meloxicam, all three tied at Tier 1 with zero restriction -- this three-way tie makes the specific 'preferred' pick an inference rather than a plan-stated designation, though the tier/restriction data itself (Tier 1, no PA/QL/ST) is read verbatim.

classes.name: SABA (Rescue)
classes.description: Short-acting beta-agonist for acute bronchospasm; quick relief, not a controller.
classes.plainDescription: The inhaler you grab when you suddenly can't breathe. Works in minutes.

classes.name: ICS (Maintenance)
classes.description: Inhaled corticosteroid; the anti-inflammatory foundation of daily asthma control.
classes.plainDescription: A daily inhaler that calms airway inflammation so attacks happen less.

classes.name: ICS/LABA (Combo)
classes.description: Steroid + long-acting bronchodilator in one inhaler for moderate-to-severe asthma or COPD.
classes.plainDescription: A daily inhaler that combines the steroid with a long-acting opener.

classes.name: LAMA
classes.description: Long-acting muscarinic antagonist. FDA-indicated for COPD maintenance, not asthma monotherapy; not for rescue.
classes.plainDescription: A once-daily opener used mainly for COPD — not a rescue inhaler.

classes.name: Biologics & Non-Inhalers
classes.description: Specialty-pharmacy biologics (anti-IgE, anti-IL5, anti-IL4) for severe asthma. Separate PA pathway; not inhalers.
classes.plainDescription: Injection or infusion drugs for severe asthma — handled differently from inhalers.

records.verificationNote: Fetched the PDF directly via curl with a browser User-Agent (fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/4/Virginia_PDL_English.pdf, 200 OK, confirmed real 153-page PDF) and extracted full text with PyPDF2. Page 1 caption confirms 'VA Cardinal Care Formulary, Medicaid-Approved Preferred Drug List, Effective May 1, 2026'. Page 17-18, under '*ANTIASTHMATIC AND BRONCHODILATOR AGENTS*' > '*ADRENERGIC COMBINATIONS***', lists (notes read verbatim): ADVAIR DISKUS (no note), ADVAIR HFA (no note), ANORO ELLIPTA (AL - excluded from this record, it is a LAMA/LABA combo not ICS/LABA), COMBIVENT RESPIMAT (no note - excluded, it is a SAMA/SABA combo), DULERA 100-5/200-5 mcg (no note), DULERA 50-5 mcg (AL), ipratropium-albuterol inhalation solution (no note - excluded, SAMA/SABA), STIOLTO RESPIMAT (AL - excluded, LAMA/LABA combo), SYMBICORT (no note), TRELEGY ELLIPTA (no note - triple ICS/LAMA/LABA therapy, included as an alternative since it contains an ICS+LABA backbone plus a LAMA). Class composition was cross-checked against known pharmacology (Advair=fluticasone/salmeterol, Dulera=mometasone/formoterol, Symbicort=budesonide/formoterol, Trelegy=fluticasone/umeclidinium/vilanterol) to correctly separate true ICS/LABA combinations from the LAMA/LABA (Anoro, Stiolto) and SAMA/SABA (Combivent, generic ipratropium-albuterol) combinations also listed under the same 'Adrenergic Combinations' heading - this classification is inferred from established drug identity, not a label printed in the PDL itself. No generic version of any ICS/LABA combination inhaler appears anywhere in this subsection or in a full-document search for 'fluticasone-salmeterol', 'budesonide-formoterol', or 'mometasone-formoterol' as generic entries - all are brand-only, hence boglActive is true by necessity (no generic exists on this PDL to prefer over brand). Symbicort was selected as the single preferredAgent since it has the broadest name recognition among the clean (no-note) options and no restriction; the other clean brands are equally covered alternatives. No class-wide step-therapy criterion appears near this section.

Page 18, under '*ANTIASTHMATIC AND BRONCHODILATOR AGENTS*' > '*BRONCHODILATORS - ANTICHOLINERGICS***', lists exactly four entries with notes read verbatim: ATROVENT HFA (PA - this is ipratropium, a short-acting muscarinic antagonist/SAMA, not a LAMA, so excluded from this record), ipratropium bromide inhalation solution (no note - also SAMA, excluded), SPIRIVA HANDIHALER (PA), SPIRIVA RESPIMAT (no note). The LAMA/LABA combination products ANORO ELLIPTA and STIOLTO RESPIMAT (both AL) and the ICS/LAMA/LABA triple TRELEGY ELLIPTA (no note), all found on page 17 under Adrenergic Combinations, are captured here as alternatives since they each contain a LAMA component (umeclidinium or tiotropium) even though none is a standalone LAMA product - this class-membership judgment (that a combination product counts as covering LAMA need) is inferred from pharmacology, not a label printed in the PDL. boglActive is true because no generic tiotropium or other LAMA exists on this PDL to prefer over the brand Spiriva Respimat. classes.name: Estrogen — transdermal
classes.description: Transdermal 17β-estradiol (patch, gel, or spray); skips first-pass hepatic metabolism, lower VTE risk than oral.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen patch or gel absorbed through the skin — gentler on clot risk than pills.

classes.name: Estrogen — oral
classes.description: Oral estradiol or conjugated estrogens; effective, but first-pass hepatic metabolism raises VTE/clot and stroke risk vs transdermal.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen pill taken by mouth. Works well, but carries a higher clot risk than the patch.

classes.name: Progestogen
classes.description: Progestogen added to systemic estrogen for endometrial protection in patients with an intact uterus; micronized progesterone preferred.
classes.plainDescription: Added to estrogen if you still have a uterus, to protect the uterine lining.

classes.name: Vaginal (local) estrogen

classes.plainDescription: Low-dose estrogen placed vaginally for dryness and urinary symptoms (GSM).

classes.name: Estrogen–progestin (oral combo)

classes.plainDescription: One pill that combines estrogen and the uterus-protecting hormone.

Read the 'ESTROGENS & PROGESTINS' drug-list table on PDF pages 81-82 (printed pages 72-73): 'estradiol transdermal patch weekly' is Tier 2, QL (4 per 28 days), no PA/ST flag; 'estradiol transdermal patch semiweekly' is Tier 2, QL (8 per 28 days), no PA/ST flag; 'dotti' (a twice-weekly estradiol transdermal patch brand) is also Tier 2, QL (8 per 28 days); 'COMBIPATCH' (estradiol-norethindrone combination patch) is Tier 3 with no requirements/limits column entry shown, so it is a non-preferred-brand-tier but still-covered alternative, not a PA/step item. No transdermal gel or spray product (e.g. Divigel, Elestrin, Evamist) appears anywhere in the OB/GYN section of the document. Cross-referenced tier/abbreviation legend on PDF pages 6 and 11. The weekly-patch generic was designated preferred over the twice-weekly variant purely for dosing convenience (both are Tier 2 with no PA) -- the formulary does not distinguish one as more 'preferred' than the other; this reflects standard clinical practice, not a payer-stated single top pick, so it is noted as inferred. No class-wide step-therapy criterion for transdermal estrogen was found in the document.

Read the 'ESTROGENS & PROGESTINS' table on PDF pages 81-82 (printed pages 72-73): 'estradiol-norethindrone acet' is Tier 1, no requirements/limits entry -- the generic equivalent of Activella, matching the class description's 'usual first-pass' generic estradiol/norethindrone acetate combo. records.tier: Preferred Drug List (PDL) - no PA required for clean-listed agents; drugs omitted from the PDL are non-preferred and generally require prior authorization under DMAS policy
records.verificationNote: Fetched the PDF directly via curl with a browser User-Agent (fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/4/Virginia_PDL_English.pdf, 200 OK, confirmed real 153-page PDF) and extracted full text with PyPDF2. Page 1 caption confirms 'VA Cardinal Care Formulary, Medicaid-Approved Preferred Drug List, Effective May 1, 2026'. Page 4-5, under '*NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENT COMBINATIONS***' and '*NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENTS (NSAIDS)***', lists the following with notes read verbatim: acetaminophen-ibuprofen (Advil Dual Action, PA; OTC; QL), diclofenac sodium oral tablet delayed release (no note), ibuprofen oral tablet 200mg (OTC; QL), ibuprofen oral tablet 300mg (PA), ibuprofen oral tablet 400mg/600mg/800mg (no note), ketoprofen oral capsule (PA; AL), meloxicam oral tablet (no note), naproxen dr oral tablet delayed release (no note), naproxen oral tablet (no note), naproxen oral tablet delayed release (no note), naproxen sodium oral capsule (Aleve, OTC), naproxen sodium oral tablet 220mg (Mediproxen, OTC), naproxen sodium oral tablet 275mg/550mg (PA), sulindac oral tablet (no note). Naproxen (generic, immediate-release 500mg-equivalent dosing) was selected as preferredAgent as the conventional first-line oral NSAID per national guidelines; the PDL applies no differential restriction to naproxen, meloxicam, sulindac, diclofenac DR, or the higher-strength ibuprofen tablets, so all are captured as alternatives with identical clean status. A full-document case-insensitive search for celecoxib/Celebrex found only one match: ELYXYB ORAL SOLUTION (PA) under '*MIGRAINE PRODUCTS - CYCLOOXYGENASE 2 (COX-2) INHIBITORS***' on page 106, a migraine-specific formulation, not a general musculoskeletal-pain NSAID line - so celecoxib is tagged nonformulary for general oral-NSAID use, not miscategorized as covered. Searches for etodolac, nabumetone, piroxicam, indomethacin, and oxaprozin returned zero matches anywhere in the document. No class-wide step-therapy criterion or BOGL note appears near this section.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Virginia Cardinal Care Medicaid-Approved Preferred Drug List, effective May 1, 2026 (fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/4/Virginia_PDL_English.pdf), page 5 'NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENTS (NSAIDS)' subsection (immediately following the single-item 'NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENT COMBINATIONS' subsection, both under the top-level 'ANALGESICS - ANTI-INFLAMMATORY' class header on pages 4-5). Full list of oral NSAID single-ingredient entries and their notes, read verbatim: ADVIL LIQUI-GELS MINIS ORAL CAPSULE (OTC), all day pain relief/all day relief (Mediproxen, OTC), childrens ibuprofen oral suspension (OTC), diclofenac sodium oral tablet delayed release (no notes), ibuprofen infants oral suspension (OTC; QL), ibuprofen oral capsule (OTC), ibuprofen oral suspension 100mg/5ml (QL) and 200mg/10ml (no notes), ibuprofen oral tablet 200mg (OTC; QL), ibuprofen oral tablet 300mg (PA), ibuprofen oral tablet 400/600/800mg (no notes), infants ibuprofen oral suspension (OTC; QL), ketoprofen oral capsule (PA; AL), meloxicam oral tablet (no notes), naproxen dr oral tablet delayed release (no notes), naproxen oral tablet (no notes, plain/immediate-release), naproxen oral tablet delayed release (no notes), naproxen sodium oral capsule / Aleve (OTC), naproxen sodium oral tablet 220mg / Mediproxen (OTC), naproxen sodium oral tablet 275mg/550mg (PA), sulindac oral tablet (no notes). Naproxen (plain oral tablet, i.e. naproxen base not the sodium salt) was selected as the preferred agent because it is the single most commonly prescribed first-line oral NSAID and carries no restriction code, consistent with meloxicam, diclofenac, standard-strength ibuprofen, and sulindac all being equally unrestricted -- the PDL applies no single 'most preferred' designation beyond the shared absence of restriction codes, so naproxen was chosen as the representative pick per this project's convention (parallels the ACE-inhibitor and diabetes classes for this same payer), not because the document singles it out. Strength (500 mg, standard naproxen base tablet dose) and sig (BID with food) are standard clinical convention, not stated in this PDL (drug/tier/PA/QL/AL/OTC notes only, no dosing directions). No class-wide step-therapy criterion or BOGL note appears in or near this section; celecoxib (Celebrex) does not appear anywhere in this PDL, consistent with it being a COX-2-selective NSAID typically managed separately or non-formulary -- not found via search, so not listed as a paRequired item since it could not be confirmed present in this document at all (absent, not merely restricted).

Confirmed line items: naproxen oral tablet 250mg (Generic, QL 6/day), 375mg (QL 4/day), 500mg (QL 3/day), plus delayed-release forms; naproxen sodium oral capsule/220mg tablet (Generic OTC), 275mg/550mg (Generic, QL 2/day); ibuprofen oral tablet 200mg (Generic OTC), 400/600/800mg (Generic, no QL); meloxicam oral tablet (Generic, no restriction); diclofenac sodium DR tablet 25mg (Generic, QL 4/day), 50/75mg (Generic, no restriction); etodolac capsule 200mg (QL 4/day), 300mg (QL 3/day), tablet (no restriction); indomethacin capsule and extended-release capsule (Generic, no restriction); oxaprozin tablet (Generic, QL 3/day); sulindac (Generic, no restriction); ketorolac oral (Generic, QL 20 per 30 days); celecoxib capsule 100mg (Generic, 'ST; QL (4 EA per 1 day)') and 200mg (Generic, 'ST; QL (2 EA per 1 day)') -- celecoxib is the only oral NSAID in this section carrying an explicit Step Therapy (ST) code. No brand-name Celebrex line appears separately (celecoxib is fully generic in this table). Naproxen 500mg is selected as the representative preferred pick because it is the most commonly-prescribed oral NSAID strength with the least restrictive QL (3/day = standard BID dosing) and zero step-therapy gate; the source table itself does not use a preferred/non-preferred column, only Generic/Brand tier + PA/ST/QL codes. Sig (500mg BID with food) is standard clinical dosing convention, not stated verbatim in this table (which lists drug/tier/requirements only, no dosing directions). No BOGL statement appears in or near this section.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the UnitedHealthcare Community Plan of Virginia (Cardinal Care) Preferred Drug List PDF (same document; fetched via curl, 200 OK, effective 7/1/2026). Section header 'Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs - Pain/Anti-Inflammatory Drugs' appears on PDF pages 236-239 (printed pages 214-217), confirmed via layout-mode extraction across all 4 pages, under the broader 'Analgesics' category. Preferred column (no PA) includes naproxen (multiple strengths and formulations), ibuprofen (400/600/800mg tablets, plus many OTC-brand generic equivalents), diclofenac sodium oral, meloxicam oral tablet (not capsule), sulindac, salsalate, and numerous aspirin products. Non-preferred/PA column is extensive: celecoxib (both brand Celebrex and its generic carry PA -- no clean-covered COX-2 option in this formulary), meloxicam capsule (vs. clean tablet), ibuprofen 300mg specifically, all diclofenac formulations other than plain oral sodium tablet, etodolac, fenoprofen, flurbiprofen, indomethacin, ketoprofen, ketorolac, meclofenamate, mefenamic acid, nabumetone, naproxen suspension/ER/combo forms, oxaprozin, piroxicam, tolmetin, and many brand-only products (Dolobid, Lodine, Orudis, Relafen, Sprix, Tolectin, Arthrotec, Cambia, Caldolor, Elyxyb, Flector). Chose naproxen 500mg oral tablet as 'the' single preferred agent per the class description's own framing (naproxen listed first among first-line oral NSAIDs) since it is clean Tier 1/no-PA in this formulary; sig/dosing reflects standard naproxen dosing for musculoskeletal pain, not stated verbatim in the PDL (drug/tier/PA/QL flags only, no dosing directions). No ST tag or class-wide step-therapy criterion found in this section. No BOGL policy found anywhere in the document.

records.verificationNote: Aetna Better Health of Virginia is a Cardinal Care Medicaid MCO and is contractually required by Virginia DMAS to follow the single statewide Common Core Formulary / Preferred Drug List for this class rather than an MCO-specific formulary. Read directly from the VA Cardinal Care Formulary Medicaid-Approved Preferred Drug List, effective May 1, 2026 (fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/4/Virginia_PDL_English.pdf, fetched via curl with a browser User-Agent, 200 OK, confirmed real 153-page PDF, text extracted with PyPDF2). Page 1 caption confirms 'VA Cardinal Care Formulary, Medicaid-Approved Preferred Drug List, Effective May 1, 2026'. Page 4-5, under 'NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENT COMBINATIONS' and 'NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENTS (NSAIDS)', lists (notes read verbatim): acetaminophen-ibuprofen (Advil Dual Action, PA; OTC; QL), diclofenac sodium oral tablet delayed release (no note), ibuprofen oral tablet 200mg (OTC; QL), ibuprofen oral tablet 300mg (PA), ibuprofen oral tablet 400mg/600mg/800mg (no note), ketoprofen oral capsule (PA; AL), meloxicam oral tablet (no note), naproxen dr oral tablet delayed release (no note), naproxen oral tablet (no note), naproxen oral tablet delayed release (no note), naproxen sodium oral capsule (Aleve, OTC), naproxen sodium oral tablet 220mg (Mediproxen, OTC), naproxen sodium oral tablet 275mg/550mg (PA), sulindac oral tablet (no note). This record is cross-verified identical to this project's own va-medicaid-ffs and anthem-healthkeepers-plus checkpoints for this class (data-gathering/va-multitopic-gather-2026-07-07/) against the same PDL. Marked partial rather than verified because this checkpoint reads the shared statewide PDL rather than an Aetna-branded document (this project's prior aetna-better-health ACE-inhibitor checkpoint used the payer-specific FormularyNavigator NDC export directly instead). Naproxen (generic, immediate-release 500mg-equivalent dosing) was selected as preferredAgent as the conventional first-line oral NSAID per national guidelines; the PDL applies no differential restriction to naproxen, meloxicam, sulindac, diclofenac DR, or the higher-strength ibuprofen tablets. A full-document search for celecoxib/Celebrex found only ELYXYB ORAL SOLUTION (PA) under Migraine Products - COX-2 Inhibitors, a migraine-specific formulation, not a general musculoskeletal-pain NSAID line - so celecoxib is tagged nonformulary for general oral-NSAID use. Searches for etodolac, nabumetone, piroxicam, indomethacin, and oxaprozin returned zero matches anywhere in the document. No class-wide step-therapy criterion or BOGL note appears near this section.

records.stepTherapy: Several non-preferred/combination NSAID products (celecoxib, diclofenac potassium capsule, diclofenac-misoprostol, ibuprofen-famotidine) carry an ST (step therapy) notation, generally requiring a trial of a Tier 1 preferred oral NSAID (e.g. naproxen, ibuprofen, meloxicam, diclofenac potassium tablet) before the plan covers the step-gated agent.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the 2026 Virginia Individual Four Tier Select Drug List, pages 9-10, 'NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENTS (NSAIDS)' subsection under 'ANALGESICS - ANTI-INFLAMMATORY' (with the related 'NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENT COMBINATIONS' subsection on page 9). Document effective date 07/01/2026 confirmed in footer. The vast majority of oral NSAIDs (naproxen, ibuprofen, meloxicam, diclofenac potassium tablet, diclofenac sodium, etodolac, indomethacin, ketorolac, nabumetone, oxaprozin, piroxicam, sulindac, flurbiprofen, mefenamic acid, meclofenamate, ketoprofen ER) are Tier 1 with QL and no PA/ST - naproxen is selected as preferred per standard first-line guidance since it and ibuprofen/meloxicam share identical Tier 1/QL-only status. Celecoxib (the only COX-2 inhibitor in this list) and diclofenac potassium capsule both carry Tier 2 + ST + QL notations, meaning step therapy is required. The combination products diclofenac-misoprostol (Tier 2, ST, QL) and ibuprofen-famotidine (Tier 3, ST, QL) also require step therapy. Note some naproxen delayed-release forms (ec-naproxen 375mg, naproxen DR 375mg) carry their own ST+QL flag even though standard naproxen/naproxen sodium tablets do not - this appears to target specific enteric-coated 375mg strengths, not the whole naproxen class.

records.verificationNote: Downloaded and read the full PDF (120 pages, SHP_MD_MEM_OMSC_230005), 'Sentara Health Employer Plans Standard Formulary for Large Group Employer Plans (July - September 2026)', page 1 confirms updated 07/01/2026. Read the 'NON-NARCOTIC ANALGESICS' drug-list table on PDF pages 36-38 (printed pages 27-29), which lists every oral NSAID alphabetically with its Drug Tier and Requirements/Limits column entry. Every generic oral NSAID found (celecoxib, diclofenac sodium DR 50mg/75mg, diclofenac topical gel, etodolac tablet, flurbiprofen, ibuprofen, indomethacin, ketorolac, meloxicam, nabumetone, naproxen (250/375/500mg IR and 375mg DR), oxaprozin, piroxicam, sulindac) is Tier 1 with a quantity-limit (QL) entry but no PA/ST flag -- i.e. clean-covered generics, all differ only by per-day quantity caps typical for chronic NSAID dosing. A smaller set (naproxen sodium 275/550mg, naproxen DR 500mg, diclofenac potassium 50mg, diclofenac sodium ER 24hr, diclofenac sodium DR 25mg, diflunisal, etodolac capsule 200/300mg and ER tablet) sits at Tier 2 with QL but still no PA/ST -- higher cost-share but still covered, so these are alternatives, never paRequired. No oral NSAID anywhere in the table carries a PA or Step Therapy flag, and no non-formulary NSAID was found. Naproxen 500mg was designated the preferred pick as the most commonly guideline-recommended first-line oral NSAID among an otherwise undifferentiated set of clean Tier-1 generics -- the source does not name one single 'preferred' NSAID over the others (they are all Tier 1), so this reflects standard clinical practice, not a payer-stated preference. Cross-referenced tier/abbreviation legend on PDF pages 6 and 11. Strength (500mg) and sig are standard naproxen dosing, not printed verbatim (the formulary lists Tier/QL only, no per-drug directions), so verification is 'verified' for tier/PA/restriction data and inferred for strength/sig. No class-wide step-therapy criterion for oral NSAIDs was found in the document.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Wellcare Value Script (PDP) 2026 Formulary (Submission ID 26195, effective 07/01/2026), pages 70-71 (Non-Narcotic Analgesics section under Analgesics). 'naproxen oral tablet 250mg, 375mg, 500mg' is listed at Tier 1 with no PA/ST/QL flag, alongside 'ibuprofen oral tablet 400mg, 600mg, 800mg' and 'meloxicam oral tablet 15mg, 7.5mg' also at Tier 1 -- all three are co-equal preferred-tier oral NSAIDs on this formulary with no restrictions. Naproxen is designated the single preferred pick here per the class's typical first-line status, though ibuprofen and meloxicam are equally unrestricted at the same tier and could be considered co-preferred. Every other oral NSAID found in the document (diclofenac in its various salts/formulations, etodolac, piroxicam, sulindac, nabumetone, celecoxib, oxaprozin, flurbiprofen, diflunisal) is clean-covered at Tier 2, 3, or 4 with no PA/ST/nonformulary flag anywhere in this section -- so the paRequired list is empty for this class on this plan.
